title: Relationships
description: Phrasal verbs about personal relationships and social interactions

points.definition: To have a friendly relationship with someone

examples.english: I get along with all my colleagues.
Do you get along with your neighbours?

points.notes: Also 'get on with' (more British). The opposite is 'not get along with'.

points.definition: To have an argument and stop being friendly with someone

examples.english: She fell out with her best friend over money.
Have you ever fallen out with a family member?

points.notes: Often followed by 'with' + person and 'over' + reason.

points.definition: To become friends again after a disagreement

examples.english: They argued last week but made up the next day.
Life is too short. You should make up with her.

points.notes: Multiple meanings! Also: 'make up a story' (invent), 'make up your mind' (decide), 'make up' (apply cosmetics).

examples.english: Many children look up to their parents.
Who do you look up to in your life?

points.notes: The opposite is 'look down on' (to think you are better than someone).

points.title: Practice: Relationships
points.description: Complete the exercises with get along with, fall out with, make up, and look up to

questions.sentence: My sister and I had a big argument, but we ______ ______ (became friends again) the next day.
questions.feedback: Correct! 'Made up' means they became friends again after the argument.

questions.sentence: I really ______ ______ ______ (admire) my grandmother. She's an amazing person.
questions.feedback: 'Look up to' means to admire and respect someone.

questions.question: Tom and his brother ______ over who should get the family house.
questions.feedback: 'Fell out' means they had an argument. 'Fell out over' tells us the reason.

questions.sentence: Do you get ______ with your new flatmate?
questions.feedback: 'Get along with' means to have a good relationship with someone.

questions.context: A: I heard you and Jake aren't talking anymore. B: Yeah, we ______ ______ last month. But I think we'll ______ ______ soon.
questions.feedback: 'Fell out' = had an argument. 'Make up' = become friends again.
Perfect: 'She gets along with everyone' - she's friendly with all people.
